questions.text: Rutherford concluded that central part of an atom is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is not correct. Rutherford's experiment showed that the central part of an atom, called the nucleus, actually has a positive charge.

This option is correct. Rutherford's experiment led him to conclude that the central part of an atom, the nucleus, is positively charged. This discovery revolutionized our understanding of atomic structure.

This option is not correct. Rutherford's experiment revealed that the majority of the atom's mass and positive charge is concentrated in the nucleus, which means it has a positive charge, not a negative charge.
This option is not correct. Based on Rutherford's experiment, the correct answer is that the central part of an atom is positively charged.

questions.explanation: (a) Electrically neutral: This option is not correct. Rutherford's experiment showed that the central part of an atom, called the nucleus, actually has a positive charge.(b) Positively charged: This option is correct. This discovery revolutionized our understanding of atomic structure.(c) Negatively charged: This option is not correct. questions.text: The first theory about the structure of an atom was introduced by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Neil Bohr, also known as Niels Bohr, proposed the Bohr model of the atom in 1913. According to his model, electrons orbit the nucleus in specific energy levels or shells. This model explained the stability of atoms and the emission and absorption of light.
While Albert Einstein made significant contributions to the field of physics, he did not introduce the first theory about the structure of an atom.
Arthur Compton was a physicist known for his work on X-ray scattering and the Compton effect, but he did not introduce the first theory about the structure of an atom.

Ernest Rutherford is the correct answer. He proposed the nuclear model of the atom in 1911. His gold foil experiment showed that atoms have a small, dense, positively charged nucleus at the center, surrounded by mostly empty space with negatively charged electrons orbiting around it.

This model explained the stability of atoms and the emission and absorption of light.(b) Einstein: While Albert Einstein made significant contributions to the field of physics, he did not introduce the first theory about the structure of an atom.(c) Compton: Arthur Compton was a physicist known for his work on X-ray scattering and the Compton effect, but he did not introduce the first theory about the structure of an atom.(d) Rutherford: Ernest Rutherford is the correct answer. He proposed the nuclear model of the atom in 1911. His gold foil experiment showed that atoms have a small, dense, positively charged nucleus at the center, surrounded by mostly empty space with negatively charged electrons orbiting around it.So, the correct answer is (d) Rutherford.

questions.text: Bohr's postulates explained by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Louis de Broglie's wave-particle duality theory was indeed influential in understanding the behavior of particles, including electrons. His theory proposed that particles can exhibit wave-like properties, which helped explain the discrete energy levels of electrons in Bohr's model.
While Albert Einstein made significant contributions to the field of physics, he did not explain Bohr's postulates.
Sir Isaac Newton's contributions to physics, such as his laws of motion and universal gravitation, are not directly related to Bohr's postulates.

questions.text: De-Broglie's wavelength is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.
Please refer to the main explanation text.

De-Broglie's wavelength equation, λ = h/p, relates the wavelength (λ) of a particle to its momentum (p) and Planck's constant (h). It suggests that every particle, including electrons, has a wave-like nature associated with it.In this equation, h represents Planck's constant, which is a fundamental constant in quantum mechanics. The momentum of a particle, represented by p, is the product of its mass and velocity.The equation tells us that the wavelength of a particle is inversely proportional to its momentum. This means that particles with higher momentum have shorter wavelengths, while particles with lower momentum have longer wavelengths.De-Broglie's wavelength equation is significant because it provides a connection between the particle and wave properties of matter. It helps us understand phenomena like electron diffraction, where electrons behave as waves and exhibit interference patterns.

questions.text: Bohr was presented atomic model of hydrogen in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In 1913, Niels Bohr introduced his atomic model for hydrogen. He suggested that electrons move in fixed orbits around the nucleus, which was a breakthrough in understanding atomic structure.
In 1919, there was a significant event called the Treaty of Versailles, which ended World War I. This treaty had major implications for the future of Europe.
In 1918, the global influenza pandemic, known as the Spanish flu, caused widespread illness and death around the world. It was a devastating time for many countries.
In 1905, Albert Einstein published his theory of special relativity, which transformed our understanding of space, time, and energy. It was a groundbreaking year for physics.

questions.text: According to Bohr theory only those orbit are allowed along which angular momentum of electron is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Please refer to the main explanation text.

In Bohr's theory, only certain electron orbits are allowed where the angular momentum of the electron is quantized, meaning it can only have specific values. The angular momentum is quantized as n times h over 2π, where n is a positive integer representing the energy level of the electron. This quantization of angular momentum was a key aspect of Bohr's model in explaining the stability of the electron orbits in an atom. It's like the electron can only dance in certain designated circles around the nucleus!

questions.text: The scientist who studies the spectrum of hydrogen in visible light wavelength range was:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Rydberg was a Swedish physicist known for the Rydberg formula, which described the spectral lines of hydrogen. His work was crucial in understanding the behavior of atomic spectra, but he didn't specifically focus on visible light wavelengths for hydrogen.

Bohr's atomic model revolutionized our understanding of atomic structure, proposing quantized electron orbits. While Bohr's model was groundbreaking for atomic physics, he didn't specifically study the visible light spectrum of hydrogen.

Balmer is the scientist who studied the spectrum of hydrogen in the visible light wavelength range. He developed the Balmer series, which focused on hydrogen's visible light emissions, making significant contributions to atomic physics in the visible light spectrum.

Paschen was a German physicist who extended the work on spectral lines beyond the Balmer series to the infrared region. His research mainly focused on the infrared spectrum of hydrogen, not the visible light range.

questions.explanation: The scientist who delved into the spectrum of hydrogen in the visible light wavelength range was actually (c) Balmer. Balmer was the brain behind the Balmer series, which specifically focused on hydrogen's visible light spectrum. He made significant contributions to understanding the light emitted by hydrogen atoms in the visible range, paving the way for further discoveries in atomic physics. It's like he shone a light on hydrogen's secrets!

questions.text: The first series, which was identified in the spectrum of hydrogen is called:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The Balmer series is the first series identified in the spectrum of hydrogen. It corresponds to transitions in hydrogen atoms that result in the emission or absorption of light in the visible range, particularly the visible light wavelength range. It's like the opening act in the hydrogen spectrum show!

The Lyman series is another important series in the hydrogen spectrum, but it's actually the ultraviolet series, not the first one identified in the visible light range. It involves transitions of electrons in hydrogen atoms that emit or absorb ultraviolet light. It's like the high-energy part of the hydrogen spectrum party!

The Paschen series is part of the infrared spectrum of hydrogen, not the first one identified in the visible light range. It deals with transitions in hydrogen atoms that emit or absorb infrared light. It's like the cozy, warm corner of the hydrogen spectrum lounge!

The Brackett series is also in the infrared spectrum, not the first one identified in the visible light range. It represents transitions in hydrogen atoms that emit or absorb infrared light beyond the Paschen series. It's like the deeper, hidden layers of the hydrogen spectrum mystery!

questions.explanation: (a) Balmer series: The Balmer series is the first series identified in the spectrum of hydrogen. It corresponds to transitions in hydrogen atoms that result in the emission or absorption of light in the visible range, particularly the visible light wavelength range. It's like the opening act in the hydrogen spectrum show!(b) Lyman series: The Lyman series is another important series in the hydrogen spectrum, but it's actually the ultraviolet series, not the first one identified in the visible light range. It involves transitions of electrons in hydrogen atoms that emit or absorb ultraviolet light. It's like the high-energy part of the hydrogen spectrum party!(c) Paschen series: The Paschen series is part of the infrared spectrum of hydrogen, not the first one identified in the visible light range. It deals with transitions in hydrogen atoms that emit or absorb infrared light. It's like the cozy, warm corner of the hydrogen spectrum lounge!(d) Brackett series: The Brackett series is also in the infrared spectrum, not the first one identified in the visible light range. It represents transitions in hydrogen atoms that emit or absorb infrared light beyond the Paschen series. It's like the deeper, hidden layers of the hydrogen spectrum mystery!So, the first series identified in the spectrum of hydrogen, specifically in the visible light wavelength range, is the Balmer series!

questions.text: The results of spectra obtained by Balmer were expressed in 1896 by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Nope, not Sir Isaac Newton this time. He made incredible contributions to physics, but the Balmer series wasn't his claim to fame in 1896.
Niels Bohr was a key player in quantum theory, but he wasn't the one expressing the results of the Balmer series in 1896.

questions.text: The process of formation of spectrum is known as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When light waves bend around obstacles or pass through small openings, that's diffraction in action. It's like light showing off its flexibility and bending skills!
This is when light waves meet and either reinforce each other (constructive interference) or cancel each other out (destructive interference). It's like a light wave dance-off!
Refraction is all about light bending when it moves from one medium to another, like from air to water. Think of how a straw looks bent in a glass of water due to refraction.
The process of formation of a spectrum is known as spectroscopy. It's like the detective work of breaking down light into its colorful components to reveal the secrets of the universe.

questions.explanation: Spectroscopy is like the CSI of light! It's all about studying how light interacts with matter. By analyzing the light that's emitted, absorbed, or scattered, scientists can uncover a wealth of information about the composition, structure, and properties of substances. It's like a magical light language that helps us decode the mysteries of the universe!

questions.text: Spectrum shows the number of component color present in certain light in terms of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is like the beat of the light wave, how fast it's waving. Different frequencies give us different colors, like the notes in a rainbow song.
Think of this as the power level of the light wave. Higher energy means bluer light, while lower energy is more on the red end of the spectrum.
Picture this as the physical distance between the light wave peaks. Shorter wavelengths mean bluer light, and longer wavelengths mean redder light.

(a) Frequency: This is like the beat of the light wave, how fast it's waving. Different frequencies give us different colors, like the notes in a rainbow song.(b) Energy: Think of this as the power level of the light wave. Higher energy means bluer light, while lower energy is more on the red end of the spectrum.(c) Wavelength: Picture this as the physical distance between the light wave peaks. Shorter wavelengths mean bluer light, and longer wavelengths mean redder light.So, when we look at the spectrum, we can see all these aspects: the frequency, energy, and wavelength of the light, painting a vibrant picture of the components present in that light. It's like reading a musical score but with colors instead of notes!

options.explanation: The transition of an electron from a higher orbit to the third orbit in a hydrogen atom corresponds to the Paschen series. The Balmer series corresponds to transitions from higher energy levels to the second energy level (n = 2). It does not involve transitions to the third orbit (n = 3), so this option is incorrect.
The Lyman series corresponds to transitions from higher energy levels to the first energy level (n = 1). It does not involve transitions to the third orbit (n = 3), so this option is incorrect.
The Brackett series corresponds to transitions from higher energy levels to the fourth energy level (n = 4). It does not involve transitions to the third orbit (n = 3), so this option is incorrect.

questions.text: An example of an absorption spectrum is the spectrum of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Sodium vapour is typically used as an example of an emission spectrum, not an absorption spectrum. It gives off bright yellow lines when excited, which is characteristic of an emission spectrum.

Atomic hydrogen's absorption spectrum contains distinct dark lines in its spectrum, which are produced when the hydrogen atoms in a gas or vapor absorb specific wavelengths of light. These dark lines represent the wavelengths at which electrons within hydrogen atoms transition from lower energy levels to higher ones. The resulting spectrum consists of these absorption lines and is crucial in understanding the energy levels and structure of hydrogen atoms. Studying the absorption spectrum of atomic hydrogen played a historic role in the development of quantum mechanics and our understanding of atomic structure.
It is not an example of absorption spectrum. Molten iron gives a continuous spectrum, because it behaves like a hot, dense source that emits light of all wavelengths when heated.
It is not the example of absorption spectrum. A mercury vapour lamp produces a line emission spectrum, showing sharp bright lines, mainly in the ultraviolet and visible range.

questions.explanation: Absorption spectrum is a type of spectrum in which electron absorbs energy from a source to go from lower energy level to higher energy level. For example, In Hydrogen spectrum, electron absorbs energy to jump from low energy level to high energy level and the spectrum obtained is called absorption spectrum.

questions.text: The radiations emitted from hydrogen filled discharge tube shows:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A continuous spectrum is a type of spectrum that contains an unbroken range of wavelengths, without any distinct lines or gaps. It is usually observed in a broad range of colors or wavelengths. Continuous spectra are typically associated with sources that emit a wide range of frequencies, such as a hot, glowing solid, liquid, or dense gas. However, the hydrogen-filled discharge tube shows distinct lines, so a continuous spectrum is not observed in this case.

A band spectrum consists of a series of bands or groups of lines that appear close together. It is typically seen in molecular spectra, where molecules can vibrate and rotate in addition to electronic transitions. However, in the case of a hydrogen-filled discharge tube, the spectrum shows individual, discrete lines rather than bands, pulling out the possibility of a band spectrum.

When the radiations emitted from a hydrogen-filled discharge tube are passed through a prism or a diffraction grating, they produce a series of discrete and well-defined lines on the spectrum. Each line corresponds to a specific wavelength of light, indicating that only certain energy levels are allowed in the hydrogen atom. This is a characteristic feature of atomic emission spectra, and it occurs when electrons in the atom transition between different energy levels.
This option is incorrect because the radiation emitted from the hydrogen-filled discharge tube does show a specific type of spectrum, which is a line spectrum, as explained above.

questions.explanation: When the radiations emitted from a hydrogen-filled discharge tube are passed through a prism or a diffraction grating, they produce a series of discrete and well-defined lines on the spectrum. Each line corresponds to a specific wavelength of light, indicating that only certain energy levels are allowed in the hydrogen atom. This is a characteristic feature of atomic emission spectra, and it occurs when electrons in the atom transition between different energy levels.

questions.text: X-rays were discovered by:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.topic: Optics, Nature of Light and Optical Instruments

options.explanation: This is the correct answer. Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen, a German physicist, discovered X-rays on November 8, 1895. He was conducting experiments with cathode-ray tubes when he observed a new kind of invisible radiation that could penetrate various materials. Roentgen named these rays 'X-rays,' and his discovery revolutionized medical imaging and had widespread applications in science and industry.

Louis de Broglie was a French physicist known for his contributions to quantum theory, particularly for introducing the concept of wave-particle duality. De Broglie's work laid the foundation for understanding the wave nature of particles, but he was not the discoverer of X-rays.
Thomas Young was a British scientist known for his double-slit experiment, which demonstrated the wave nature of light. However, he did not discover X-rays.
Albert A. Michelson was an American physicist known for his work on the precise measurement of the speed of light and for the Michelson interferometer. He was not involved in the discovery of X-rays.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is A. Wilhelm Conrad Roentgen discovered X-rays on November 8, 1895, while experimenting with cathode-ray tubes. He observed a new form of invisible radiation that could penetrate various materials, naming it 'X-rays.' Roentgen's discovery had a significant impact on medical imaging and various scientific and industrial applications.Option B, De Broglie, is incorrect as he was known for his work on wave-particle duality in quantum theory, not for discovering X-rays. Option C, Young, is incorrect as Thomas Young was famous for his double-slit experiment demonstrating the wave nature of light, not X-rays. Option D, Michelson, is incorrect as Albert A. Michelson's contributions were in measuring the speed of light precisely and developing interferometers, not in the discovery of X-rays.
